issue_title: Agreements will not receive payment.sale.completed webhook if created before 8/23/16
issue_body:
## Issue Description
Via #132 [(Specific comment here...)](https://github.com/paypal/PayPal-Python-SDK/issues/132#issuecomment-251130368)
> @ppmtscory  ...the billing agreement ID would be included in the PAYMENT.SALE.COMPLETED webhook. These events will only be sent for subscriptions that were set up after this went live which was towards the end of Aug...

What this means is that currently in order for an existing application to use this webhook:

1) The application will need to cancel their previous subscriptions
2) Notify their customers that their subscriptions have been canceled and that they will need to re-execute a new one
3) Their customers have to actually take the effort to go re-execute before the next payment cycle

If the customer doesn't follow through, the application will lose revenue.

I have not seen this documented anywhere previously and this webhook has been live for 6 weeks at this point. 

This issue may also result in a false positive because setting up new agreements during testing can result in expected behavior but previous agreements in production environments will be affected.

### General information

* SDK/Library version: 1.13.1
* Environment: Sandbox & production
* `PayPal-Debug-ID` values: N/A
* Language, language version, and OS: Python 3.6.3, Ubuntu 16.04

### Issue description

When `payment.execute` fails and returns `False`, how can we get more info about what happened? [The sample code](https://github.com/paypal/PayPal-Python-SDK/blob/master/samples/payment/execute.py) says to use `payment.error`. At least for me that raises a `TypeError: 'dict' object is not callable`. Doing `payment['error']` instead raises a `KeyError`. I had a look at the `payment` object and it doesn't look like there is any error information in it.

Any other way to obtain that information?

https://github.com/PUNCH-Cyber/YaraGuardian/commits/4c2e2d8a8c5cf0380b8d96207d5be0ffd7b98ede/plyara/plyara/plyara.py

Some of these are improvements not included in the current codebase. We should import what we can.

This is a really useful feature. I was thinking of the following scenario not necessarily captured.

Imagine you have a C++ object with an iterator range constructor 

``Foo::Foo( Iterator begin, Iterator end) { ..}``

Now whenever you have a type that is also a range over ``Iterator::value_type`` you can offer a conversion from that type to Foo.

I suppose this can also be solved by offering an explicit Range constructor for Foo, e.g.

```
template< typename Range>
Foo( const Range & r){ ... }
```

Then you can always implicitly convert? 

However, is it certainly seems possible to codify ``implicit_range_convert<A,B>(){}`` which glues conversion code in to the iterator range constructor.

This would have the value that you could imagine ``implementing implicit_range_covert<B>() {}`` which then automatically glues all known valid range types..

Thoughts?

issue_title: Remove template_file option for PBS configuration
issue_body:
We have two options, `job_template` for inline template (using multi-line yaml format) and `template_file` for using external file. The reason for the addition of `template_file` was that

1. It is possible for multiple task queues to sharing the same template file
2. Multi-line YAML string can be difficult to enter, especially from command line using command `sos config`.

The problems with `template_file` are
1. It makes `hosts.yml` less portable.
2. It is difficult to make changes to the included file with the change of other options for the task queue.

I have however found that it is relatively easy to include templates inside `hosts.yml`, perhaps it is better to remove the `template_file` option to keep things simple.

issue_title: # MergeLayer fails in python 3 when input layers have shapes containing None
issue_body:
The function `autocrop_array_shapes` (lasagne/layers/merge.py) raises a `TypeError` in python 3 when any of the `input_shapes` have any elements of type `NoneType`.

Sample code:

```
from lasagne.layers.merge import autocrop_array_shapes

# Works for python 2 and 3
shape_no_crop = autocrop_array_shapes([(None, 2, 3, 4), (5, 6, 7, 8)], cropping=None)
print("Shape with no cropping:")
print(shape_no_crop)

# Works for python 2, fails for python 3
shape_crop = autocrop_array_shapes([(None, 2, 3, 4), (5, 6, 7, 8)], cropping=["center"]*4)
print("Shape with cropping:")
print(shape_crop)
```

python2.7 output:

```
Shape with no cropping:
[(None, 2, 3, 4), (5, 6, 7, 8)]
Shape with cropping:
[(None, 2, 3, 4), (None, 2, 3, 4)]
```

python3.4 output/stack trace:

```
Shape with no cropping:
[(None, 2, 3, 4), (5, 6, 7, 8)]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/magicpony/Lasagne/examples/merge_layer_bug.py", line 12, in <module>
    shape_crop = autocrop_array_shapes([(None, 2, 3, 4), (5, 6, 7, 8)], cropping=["center"]*4)
  File "/home/magicpony/Lasagne/lasagne/layers/merge.py", line 236, in autocrop_array_shapes
    result.append([min(sh)] * len(sh))
TypeError: unorderable types: int() < NoneType()
```

I am using:
 - Python 2.7/3.4
 - Lasagne Master (d1d7843a346c8182df26b1d83cf43b6b7d4ba78b)
 - Theano 0.8.0

This seems to be caused by the changes between python 2 and 3 in comparing objects of different types. In python 2, `int() < None` evaluates to `False`. In python 3 this raises a `TypeError: unorderable types` (same goes for `None < None`).

It seems to me that the behaviour of `autocrop_array_shapes` in python 2 is expected.
(i.e. if the shape for a particular axis is `None` for any of the inputs, `None` should be returned for the shape after cropping).


To set some context within a more practical use case, here is an example that behaves as expected in python 2 but raises the exception in python 3 (using a `MergeLayer` with an `InputLayer` for which `shape=(None, 4, None, None)`.

```
import numpy
import theano
import theano.tensor as T
from lasagne.layers import InputLayer, Conv2DLayer, get_output
from lasagne.layers.merge import ElemwiseSumLayer

inputTensor1 = T.tensor4("input1")
inputTensor2 = T.tensor4("input2")

imgSize = 20
inputLayer0 = InputLayer(shape=(None, 4, None, None), name="inputLayer1")
inputLayer1 = InputLayer(shape=(None, 4, None, None), name="inputLayer2")

inputData0 = numpy.ones((10, 4, imgSize, imgSize))
inputData1 = numpy.ones((10, 4, imgSize + 2, imgSize -2))

# Merge layer followed by conv layer:
net =  ElemwiseSumLayer([inputLayer0, inputLayer1], cropping=["lower"] * 4)
net = Conv2DLayer(net, num_filters=4, filter_size=3)

inputs = [inputTensor1, inputTensor2]
output = get_output(net, inputs={inputLayer0: inputs[0], inputLayer1: inputs[1]})
predictFunction = theano.function(inputs, output)

outputData = predictFunction(inputData0, inputData1)
print("Shape of output data:")
print(outputData.shape)

print("Expected shape:")
print(10, 4, 18, 16)
```

I think this can be fixed so that it works in both python 2 and 3 by replacing line 236 of lasagne/layers/merge.py (in `autocrop_array_shapes` ):
```
result.append([min(sh)] * len(sh))
```
with:
```
min_sh = None if any(x is None for x in sh) else min(sh)
result.append([min_sh] * len(sh))
```
If this is the desired behaviour, I'm happy to add some tests and submit a PR.

Would it be possible to add a kwarg to filter the output of get_events?
I know that it can be done via list comprehension but that kinda defeats the purpose of having get_events as a generator.

Is it possible to extend the Product.Save() function beyond the basic boolean? Possibility using error codes such as the HTTP coding format?

I have two products, and one when saved is returned true, and the other false. The products are complete in their data structures. A response code would be easier on the troubleshooting of why the second product fails.

Thanks

issue_title: Potential new required dependency for lexicon.
issue_body:
CC: @bmw 

Hey @certbot

I wanted to give you guys a heads up that [AnalogJ/lexicon](https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon) will have a new top level dependency once the Google Cloud DNS provider is merged in https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/pull/261

Basically the idea is to add `cryptography` as a top level dependency so that developers implementing providers that utilize OAuth will have a library available to do most of the heavy lifting for them. 

I know lexicon dependency changes have broken your build/CI process in the past, so I wanted to give you guys a heads up and see how you guys wanted me to proceed doing the merge & release. 

Also, if you guys have any feedback about the Google Cloud DNS provider, you can comment here: https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/pull/261
Part of #5409.

issue_title: diff flags don't appear to do anything for Index.diff_to_tree
issue_body:
(previously filed as [issue #3859 on libgit2](https://github.com/libgit2/libgit2/issues/3859))

I'm using `pygit2.__version__=='0.24.1'`, and I've found that the `GIT_DIFF_*` constants don't appear to have _any_ effect at all on `index.diff_to_tree`.

Am I missing something here? Is this a bug?

For example, here's a snippet that shows identical diffs with and without the `GIT_DIFF_REVERSE` flag:

```python
import pygit2
from time import time

repo = pygit2.init_repository('/tmp/%d.git'%time(), bare=True)

blob = repo.create_blob('blah blah blah\nx y z\n')
repo.index.add( pygit2.IndexEntry('foo.txt', blob, pygit2.GIT_FILEMODE_BLOB) )
ic = repo.create_commit('refs/heads/master', repo.default_signature, repo.default_signature, 'initial commit', repo.index.write_tree(), [])

blob2 = repo.create_blob('blah blah blah\nx y z\na b c')
repo.index.clear()
repo.index.add( pygit2.IndexEntry('foo.txt', blob2, pygit2.GIT_FILEMODE_BLOB) )

diff = repo.index.diff_to_tree( repo[ic].tree )
print("Forward diff")
print(diff.patch)

diffr = repo.index.diff_to_tree( repo[ic].tree, flags=pygit2.GIT_DIFF_REVERSE)
print("Reverse diff")
print(diff.patch)
```

issue_title: Archived non-absolute HTTP redirects are not replayed correctly
issue_body:
According to [RFC7231](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-7.1.2), the HTTP location response header may be a URI fragment, e.g., Location: `/foo.html`.

When replaying a redirect from http://localhost:5000/memento/20180727203127/example.com/ to http://localhost:5000/memento/20180727203127/example.com/anotherURI as in https://github.com/oduwsdl/ipwb/blob/master/ipwb/samples/warcs/redirectRelative.warc, the user is instead redirected to http://localhost:5000/memento/20180727203127//anotherURI.

This has to do with the logic of URI resolution to the replay system. Ideally, the ServiceWorker would handle this but it does not appear to do so.

issue_title: Luigi doesn't check default arguments when deciding whether a job is already running
issue_body:
I have a job with a `date` CLI argument that defaults to yesterday. It's cronned to run daily.

However, if there's a problem with the job and it doesn't terminate for one day, then the next day when the command is run, luigi reports that the job is already running, when in fact it isn't because the `date` parameter will now be different.

E.g.:

The job is invoked on 2015-03-30 with `my-job --another-arg=blah`. The date parameter is not explicitly passed so defaults to the day before, 2015-03-29. This job fails and retry logic keeps it around.
The next night, 2015-03-31, cron executes `my-job --another-arg=blah` again, and this time the `date` parameter defaults to the value 2015-03-30. Luigi should run this job but it doesn't. Instead it reports that the job is already running and exits. 

Obviously the fix for now is to explicitly pass the `date` parameter, but really luigi should calculate the values of all default values before deciding whether a job is already running or not.

issue_title: Combining Klein with other asynchronous Python libraries
issue_body:
Dear Twisted Klein-Team!

thank you for making Klein available! It has the charming interface of Flask but keeps everything asnychronous.

We would like to combine Klein with a python library for bus communication. The following code works fine for us without Klein.

```python
"""Example for switching a light on and off."""
import asyncio
from xknx import XKNX
from xknx.devices import Light

async def main():
    """Connect to KNX/IP bus, switch on light, wait 2 seconds and switch of off again."""
    xknx = XKNX()
    light = Light(xknx,
                      name='VarBool',
                      group_address_switch='2/1/1') 
    await xknx.start() 
 
    i = 0
    while i<5:
        i = i + 1
        print ("Turn on, wait 1 s")
        await light.set_on()
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
        print ("Turn of wait 1s")
        await light.set_off()
        await asyncio.sleep(1)
    await xknx.stop()    

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())
loop.close()
```

This is our attempt to integrate xknx and Klein:

```python

import treq
from klein import Klein
from xknx import XKNX
from xknx.devices import Light
import asyncio

app = Klein()

@app.route('/on', [ 'GET' ])
async def on(request):
    xknx = XKNX()
    light = Light(xknx,
                  name='VarBool',
                 group_address_switch='2/1/1')
    await xknx.start()
    await light.set_on()
    await xknx.stop()

    return 'Light is on'


@app.route('/off', branch=True)
def off(request):
    xknx = XKNX()
    light = Light(xknx,
                  name='VarBool',
                  group_address_switch='2/1/1')
    await xknx.start()
    await light.set_off()
    await xknx.stop()
    
    return 'Light is off'

app.run("localhost", 8080)
```
Unfortunately this does not work. We tried different things but did not come up with a solution. We usually get an error "error.BuiltIn - No yield from provided for Future".

It would be great if you might suggest how to integrate other asynchronous python code.

Thanks

Georg

issue_title: Automatically clear ERROR register charge or discharge started or stopped by script
issue_body: Currently, the error register is not cleared. Thus, we run into situations where you move to 'STOPPED' mode using the script, and then accidentally think there was an error somewhere because the ERROR register is still carrying around error information from previous charge and discharge events.

issue_title: z3 hangs when push/pop is used
issue_body:
Hi,

I've come across the following issue in z3 (built from master/HEAD): when solving the following formula, z3 prints 'unsat' immediately on the first check-sat call, but then hangs on the second one. STP is able to quickly generate an unsat result for both, and I don't think the issue is one of complexity in the formula itself. 

```
(set-logic QF_BV)

(declare-const ZERO32 (_ BitVec 32))
(assert (= ZERO32 #x00000000))
(declare-const MAXU32 (_ BitVec 64))
(assert (= MAXU32 #x00000000ffffffff))

(declare-fun elementByteSize () (_ BitVec 32))
(declare-fun numElements () (_ BitVec 32))
(declare-fun div_res () (_ BitVec 32))
(declare-fun totalSize () (_ BitVec 32))
(declare-fun big_ElementByteSize () (_ BitVec 64))
(declare-fun big_NumElements () (_ BitVec 64))
(declare-fun calloc_size () (_ BitVec 64))

(assert (not (= elementByteSize ZERO32)))

(assert (and
	(= big_NumElements ((_ zero_extend 32) numElements))
	(= big_ElementByteSize ((_ zero_extend 32) elementByteSize))
	(= calloc_size (bvmul big_NumElements big_ElementByteSize))
	(bvugt calloc_size MAXU32)
))

(push)
(assert (= numElements ZERO32))
(check-sat)

(pop)
(assert (and
	(not (= numElements ZERO32))
	(= totalSize (bvmul numElements elementByteSize))
	(= div_res (bvudiv totalSize numElements))
	(= div_res elementByteSize)
))
(check-sat)
```

If I'm doing something obviously wrong let me know =) 

Thanks,
Sean

When building Z3 with UBSan, when the "interpolation_example" runs the following errors are reported.

/home/user/z3_src/src/interp/iz3mgr.h:99:13: runtime error: member call on null pointer of type 'ast_manager'
```

This is for 110d558ee4262721f3ab2c999d789eaf84ac15d6

In d7e6ca763f0f971da5e21ffd952128e29b4bf119

After successful make, install doesn't work 

```
dejan@pup:~/workspace/z3-unstable/build$ make install
cp: cannot stat ‘libz3java.so’: No such file or directory
make: *** [install] Error 1
```

Configured with 

```
python scripts/mk_make.py --prefix=/homes/dejan/Software
```

```python
import z3
a = z3.BitVec('', 16)
print a
```

This code produces a parser error:

```
from z3 import *
f = 3.00146118189147e+16
s = str(f)
RealVal(s)  # z3.z3types.Z3Exception: parser error
RealVal(f)  # z3.z3types.Z3Exception: parser error
```
Same with `-f`.

It behaves differently with floats and ints:
```
RealVal(30014611818914700)       # ok
RealVal(30014611818914700.0)    # z3.z3types.Z3Exception: parser error
RealVal('30014611818914700.0')  # ok
```

In this example Z3 seems to get into an infinite loop in proto_model::eval, quickly exhausting memory. I would recommend running this in the debug version. Otherwise you have to kill it quickly to avoid thrashing your machine. It builds up a very long 'todo' list in proto_model::eval, which makes me think it might have built a cyclic expression node. If you remove the line '(push)' Z3 finishes quickly with a SAT result. 

repro: see the attached input file (and remove the .txt extension)
 
[infinite_loop.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1733079/infinite_loop.smt2.txt)

given you can now set the conn + read timeouts in ClientSession, and the major changes in 2.x it seems like it would have been nice to set the default conn/read timeouts in clientsession to be something like `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT` and then set the default `_request` timeout to None.  Thoughts?  Sorry I didn't think about this earlier.

issue_body:
Trying to follow the direction in the readme to retrain the model and I get the following exception...

```
$ parserator label name_data/unlabeled/banks.csv name_data/labeled/banks_labeled.xml probablepeople
~/probablepeople/probablepeople/__init__.py:66: UserWarning: You must train the model (parserator train [traindata] [modulename]) to create the person_learned_settings.crfsuite file before you can use the parse and tag methods
  warnings.warn('You must train the model (parserator train [traindata] [modulename]) to create the %s file before you can use the parse and tag methods' % MODEL_FILES[model_type])
~/probablepeople/probablepeople/__init__.py:66: UserWarning: You must train the model (parserator train [traindata] [modulename]) to create the company_learned_settings.crfsuite file before you can use the parse and tag methods
  warnings.warn('You must train the model (parserator train [traindata] [modulename]) to create the %s file before you can use the parse and tag methods' % MODEL_FILES[model_type])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "~/.env/bin/parserator", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(dispatch())
  File "~/.env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/parserator/main.py", line 58, in dispatch
    args.func(args)
  File "~/.env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/parserator/main.py", line 79, in label
    manual_labeling.label(module, infile_path, outfile_path)
  File "~/.env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/parserator/manual_labeling.py", line 213, in label
    if module.TAGGER:
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'TAGGER'

```

Seems to be a bug related to #25

Can you please provide more documentation on`RecordLink.blocker()` and related methods?

Looking at the source, it seems that what happens is:

1) In `_blockData`, `data_2` gets indexed and then blocked with `target=True`.
2) In `_blockGenerator` you block data_1 (which is confusingly referred to as `messy_data` even though in this context both datasets are clean) with `target=False`.
3) You generate blocks containing one record from data_1 and all records of data_2 that share any of its block keys.
4) You seem to manually assign empty set to all of the covered blocks sets for data_2 records.

My concrete questions are:

A) what does the `target` argument to `blocker()` actually do?
B) why don't you have to do `self.blocker.indexAll` on `data_1` a.k.a. `messy_data` before blocking it, like you do with `data_2`?
C) why are the covered block sets empty? couldn't a data_2 record have appeared in an earlier block? i'm looking at the [`matchBlocks()` documentation](https://dedupe.io/developers/library/en/latest/API-documentation.html#RecordLink.matchBlocks) for my understanding of the covered blocks set.
D) Gazeteer seems to inherit all of this. Can you point me to where the Gazetteer logic differs from RecordLink to allow for multiple records from `messy_data` to match one record from `data_2`?

Thanks!

issue_title: [TEST] Provide Chain Compatibility Test Cases and Skeletons
issue_body:
Affects #549, and web3.py in general

### What is wrong?

Issue #549 asks the author of the solution to provide the test-cases:

>To be complete:
>    A test showing that a long extradata does not raise an error, after adding the new middleware
>    A test showing that a long extradata does raise an error in the default configuration

The issue is:

* a) Those test-cases should be available already
* b) Test-code should be ideally not written by the person which wrote the code it tests

### How can it be fixed?

Can someone who is familiar with the test-system provide the relevant tests, ideally within a test-subgroup of the kind "chain-compatibility"?

This one test would be enough:

> A test showing that a long extradata does raise an error in the default configuration

### What was wrong?

The `pyethereum` codebase is unmaintained.

### How can it be fixed?

Drop both of the pyethereum based backends.

issue_title: Debian, Ubuntu, Fedora and nix ship outdated packages
issue_body:
According to https://repology.org/metapackage/vdirsyncer/versions, Debian unstable ships with 0.14.0 (October 2016), Fedora rawhide with 0.14.1 (three weeks old), nixpkgs with 0.11.3 (half a year!!)

@pimutils/packaging Please make sure you are subscribed to something like https://github.com/pimutils/vdirsyncer/releases.atom or the PyPI releases and act accordingly. If you ship a version of vdirsyncer that contains bugs fixed last year, I'd much rather see that package completely removed.

We have a case where we hit this assertion on windows: https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial-asyncio/blob/master/serial_asyncio/__init__.py#L121

Can somebody explain the rational behind this assertion to me? n == 0 seems totally legit. Internally, os.write is called which may return 0. This seems to never happen on linux but on windows. 

In my opinion the assert should be like this:
`assert n >= 0 < len(data)`

Same probably applies to this line: https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial-asyncio/blob/master/serial_asyncio/__init__.py#L262

This is the first project I've ever seen to produce command line garble because an **optional** is not there.

You likely know I am talking about [this](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/blob/master/fuzzywuzzy/fuzz.py#L33).

#67 was closed after a mere package suggestion. @medecau made a very solid point: this pertains to the documentation, if it is optional, do not throw it at every terminal that will use the code.

I can throw in a PR removing that if you don't want to write it. It could maybe just be logged silently at a very high level.

issue_title: Logging and swallowing of exceptions causes false-postitive test results
issue_body:
https://github.com/yunojuno/django-side-effects/blob/0c1a01f029a4862babcb0820b02ea3b59a9407fc/side_effects/registry.py#L94  swallows all exceptions and sends them to the `logger` as an `exception()`.

This is arguably OK in production (though am not a fan, personally), insomuch as a site with properly configured logging will catch this and inform the maintainers who will/should fix it ASAP (and side effects may be deemed acceptable to lose).

However, unit tests don't benefit from this, and so will still register a pass, even though the side effect for a decorated method or function is failing -- denying developers who aren't reading their test run output closely the chance to fix it before it hits production. I've seen this happen.

As an example, here's some output from a a test which uses a side-effect to send an email, which fails because the template is missing, but which passes in test terms, because the exception is caught. 

```
test_submit_something_to_someone (myproj.tests.apps.myapp.test_models.USFreelancerExternalComplianceStateChangingTests) ... ERROR    Error running side_effect function 'myproj.apps.myapp.side_effects.email.submit_something_to_someone'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/python_user/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/lib/python3.6/site-packages/side_effects/registry.py", line 92, in _run_func
    func(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/app/myproj/apps/myapp/side_effects/email.py", line 34, in submit_something_to_someone
    context={}
  File "/app/myproj/apps/messaging/email2.py", line 44, in send_mail
    email_queue.enqueue(_send_mail, template_name, context, **kwargs)
  File "/home/python_user/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/lib/python3.6/site-packages/rq/queue.py", line 280, in enqueue
    job_id=job_id, at_front=at_front, meta=meta)
  File "/home/python_user/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django_rq/queues.py", line 60, in enqueue_call
    return self.original_enqueue_call(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/python_user/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django_rq/queues.py", line 56, in original_enqueue_call
    return super(DjangoRQ, self).enqueue_call(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/python_user/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/lib/python3.6/site-packages/rq/queue.py", line 232, in enqueue_call
    job = self.enqueue_job(job, at_front=at_front)
  File "/home/python_user/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/lib/python3.6/site-packages/rq/queue.py", line 307, in enqueue_job
    job = self.run_job(job)
  File "/home/python_user/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/lib/python3.6/site-packages/rq/queue.py", line 237, in run_job
    job.perform()
  File "/home/python_user/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/lib/python3.6/site-packages/rq/job.py", line 560, in perform
    self._result = self._execute()
  File "/home/python_user/.pyenv/versions/3.6.1/lib/python3.6/site-packages/rq/job.py", line 566, in _execute
    return self.func(*self.args, **self.kwargs)
  File "/app/myproj/apps/messaging/email2.py", line 62, in _send_mail
    email = template.create_message(context, **kwargs)
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'create_message'
ok
```

So, because that AttributeError is swallowed even though it's complaining about something that effectively breaks production, I'd call that a false positive.

Any chance we can have a `settings.SIDE_EFFECTS_RAISE_EXCEPTIONS` config option or similar, so that tests (and production if you want it) can be fail hard?

For example, when the field value is null it would give the following error [on this line](https://github.com/Axiologue/DjangoRestMultipleModels/blob/master/drf_multiple_model/mixins.py#L157): `unorderable types: NoneType() < str()`. In my case I'm using `DateTimeField` type as the sorting field, which can contain `None` sometimes.
Also, it would be very useful once it has a support for specifying multiple sorting fields.

issue_title: Test suite is broken for Python 3.x
issue_body:
The module `test.test_support` exists only in Python 2. This was renamed to `test.support` in Python 3. I am shipping the following patch in the Debian package for this module:

```
Description: Use test.support if Python 3 to enable test suite on Python 3
Author: Iain R. Learmonth <irl@debian.org>
Last-Update: 2016-09-23
---
--- a/tests/applicationinsights_tests/TestTelemetryClient.py
+++ b/tests/applicationinsights_tests/TestTelemetryClient.py
@@ -1,10 +1,15 @@
+import sys
+import os
+import os.path
 import unittest
 import inspect
 import json
 
-from test import test_support
+if sys.version_info < (3, 0):
+    from test import test_support
+else:
+    from test import support as test_support
 
-import sys, os, os.path
 rootDirectory = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__)), '..', '..')
 if rootDirectory not in sys.path:
     sys.path.append(rootDirectory)
```

issue_title: Help regarding using the adversarial attacks with foolbox using tensorflow
issue_body:
I have trained mobilenet from scratch using a custom dataset (grayscale images) . Here's the test.py which takes arguments as the dataset directory(the tfrecord files) and the logdir directory containing the saved model (.ckpt file ) , the meta and the index files. Can you please guide me how to use this tensorflow code along with foolbox to use different attacks on grayscale images(299*299)  and obtain the accuracy ?  I have tried a lot but errors have come up every time. Can you please provide a working code with the help of the mentioned code below? 
```
from __future__ import absolute_import
from __future__ import division
from __future__ import print_function

import argparse
import os

import numpy as np
import tensorflow as tf
import tensorflow.contrib.slim as slim

from config.parse_config import parse_config_file
from nets import nets_factory
from preprocessing import inputs

def test(tfrecords, checkpoint_path, save_dir, max_iterations, eval_interval_secs, cfg, read_images=False):
    """
    Args:
        tfrecords (list)
        checkpoint_path (str)
        savedir (str)
        max_iterations (int)
        cfg (EasyDict)
    """
    tf.logging.set_verbosity(tf.logging.DEBUG)

    graph = tf.Graph()

    with graph.as_default():

        global_step = slim.get_or_create_global_step()

        with tf.device('/cpu:0'):
            batch_dict = inputs.input_nodes(
                tfrecords=tfrecords,
                cfg=cfg.IMAGE_PROCESSING,
                num_epochs=1,
                batch_size=cfg.BATCH_SIZE,
                num_threads=cfg.NUM_INPUT_THREADS,
                shuffle_batch =cfg.SHUFFLE_QUEUE,
                random_seed=cfg.RANDOM_SEED,
                capacity=cfg.QUEUE_CAPACITY,
                min_after_dequeue=cfg.QUEUE_MIN,
                add_summaries=False,
                input_type='test',
                read_filenames=read_images
            )

            batched_one_hot_labels = slim.one_hot_encoding(batch_dict['labels'],
                                                        num_classes=cfg.NUM_CLASSES)

        arg_scope = nets_factory.arg_scopes_map[cfg.MODEL_NAME]()

        with slim.arg_scope(arg_scope):
            logits, end_points = nets_factory.networks_map[cfg.MODEL_NAME](
                inputs=batch_dict['inputs'],
                num_classes=cfg.NUM_CLASSES,
                is_training=False
            )

            predictions = end_points['Predictions']
            #labels = tf.squeeze(batch_dict['labels'])
            labels = batch_dict['labels']

            # Add the loss summary
            loss = tf.losses.softmax_cross_entropy(
                logits=logits, onehot_labels=batched_one_hot_labels, label_smoothing=0., weights=1.0)

        if 'MOVING_AVERAGE_DECAY' in cfg and cfg.MOVING_AVERAGE_DECAY > 0:
            variable_averages = tf.train.ExponentialMovingAverage(
                cfg.MOVING_AVERAGE_DECAY, global_step)
            variables_to_restore = variable_averages.variables_to_restore(
                slim.get_model_variables())
            variables_to_restore[global_step.op.name] = global_step
        else:
            variables_to_restore = slim.get_variables_to_restore()
            variables_to_restore.append(global_step)


        # Define the metrics:
        metric_map = {
            'Accuracy': tf.metrics.accuracy(labels=labels, predictions=tf.argmax(predictions, 1)),#slim.metrics.streaming_accuracy(labels=labels, predictions=tf.argmax(predictions, 1)),
            loss.op.name : slim.metrics.streaming_mean(loss)
        }
        if len(cfg.ACCURACY_AT_K_METRIC) > 0:
            bool_labels = tf.ones([cfg.BATCH_SIZE], dtype=tf.bool)
            for k in cfg.ACCURACY_AT_K_METRIC:
                if k <= 1 or k > cfg.NUM_CLASSES:
                    continue
                in_top_k = tf.nn.in_top_k(predictions=predictions, targets=labels, k=k)
                metric_map['Accuracy_at_%s' % k] = tf.metrics.accuracy(labels=bool_labels, predictions=in_top_k)#slim.metrics.streaming_accuracy(labels=bool_labels, predictions=in_top_k)

        names_to_values, names_to_updates = slim.metrics.aggregate_metric_map(metric_map)

        if max_iterations > 0:
            num_batches = max_iterations
        else:
            # This ensures that we make a single pass over all of the data.
            # We could use ceil if the batch queue is allowed to pad the last batch
            num_batches = np.floor(cfg.NUM_TEST_EXAMPLES / float(cfg.BATCH_SIZE))


        sess_config = tf.ConfigProto(
            log_device_placement=cfg.SESSION_CONFIG.LOG_DEVICE_PLACEMENT,
            allow_soft_placement = True,
            gpu_options = tf.GPUOptions(
                per_process_gpu_memory_fraction=cfg.SESSION_CONFIG.PER_PROCESS_GPU_MEMORY_FRACTION
            ),
            intra_op_parallelism_threads=cfg.SESSION_CONFIG.INTRA_OP_PARALLELISM_THREADS if 'INTRA_OP_PARALLELISM_THREADS' in cfg.SESSION_CONFIG else None,
            inter_op_parallelism_threads=cfg.SESSION_CONFIG.INTER_OP_PARALLELISM_THREADS if 'INTER_OP_PARALLELISM_THREADS' in cfg.SESSION_CONFIG else None
        )

            if os.path.isdir(checkpoint_path):
                checkpoint_dir = checkpoint_path
                checkpoint_path = tf.train.latest_checkpoint(checkpoint_dir)

                if checkpoint_path is None:
                    raise ValueError("Unable to find a model checkpoint in the " \
                                     "directory %s" % (checkpoint_dir,))

            tf.logging.info('Evaluating %s' % checkpoint_path)

            slim.evaluation.evaluate_once(
                master='',
                checkpoint_path=checkpoint_path,
                logdir=save_dir,
                num_evals=num_batches,
                eval_op=names_to_updates.values(),
                variables_to_restore=variables_to_restore,
                session_config=sess_config
            )

def parse_args():

    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Test the person classifier')

    parser.add_argument('--tfrecords', dest='tfrecords',
                        help='Paths to tfrecords.', type=str,
                        nargs='+', required=True)

    parser.add_argument('--checkpoint_path', dest='checkpoint_path',
                          help='Path to a specific model to test against. If a directory, then the newest checkpoint file will be used.', type=str,
                          required=True, default=None)

    parser.add_argument('--save_dir', dest='savedir',
                          help='Path to directory to store summary files.', type=str,
                          required=True)

    parser.add_argument('--config', dest='config_file',
                        help='Path to the configuration file.',
                        required=True, type=str)

    parser.add_argument('--eval_interval_secs', dest='eval_interval_secs',
                        help='Go into an evaluation loop, waiting this many seconds between evaluations. Default is to evaluate once.',
                        required=False, type=int, default=0)

    parser.add_argument('--batch_size', dest='batch_size',
                        help='The number of images in a batch.',
                        required=False, type=int, default=None)

    parser.add_argument('--batches', dest='batches',
                        help='Maximum number of iterations to run. Default is all records (modulo the batch size).',
                        required=False, type=int, default=0)

    parser.add_argument('--model_name', dest='model_name',
                        help='The name of the architecture to use.',
                        required=False, type=str, default=None)

    parser.add_argument('--read_images', dest='read_images',
                        help='Read the images from the file system using the `filename` field rather than using the `encoded` field of the tfrecord.',
                        action='store_true', default=False)

    args = parser.parse_args()
    return args

def main():

    args = parse_args()

    cfg = parse_config_file(args.config_file)

    if args.batch_size != None:
        cfg.BATCH_SIZE = args.batch_size

    if args.model_name != None:
        cfg.MODEL_NAME = args.model_name

    test(
        tfrecords=args.tfrecords,
        checkpoint_path=args.checkpoint_path,
        save_dir=args.savedir,
        max_iterations=args.batches,
        eval_interval_secs=args.eval_interval_secs,
        cfg=cfg,
        read_images=args.read_images
    )

if __name__ == '__main__':
main()
```

issue_title: Add "frame" member to ObservationResult to be backwards compatible

issue_body:
I've had some issues with using `clickhouse_driver` in a multithreaded asyncio environment. The connection seems not to be thread safe. I'm not sure that is the best approach to solve the issues I've encountered, but here is a pooled connection implementation I am using:
```
import multiprocessing
import asyncio
import logging
from functools import partial
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

from clickhouse_driver import Client
import threading

class ThreadSafeClient:
    def __init__(self, host, compression):
        self._host = host
        self._compression = compression
        self._loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self._pool = {}

    async def get_conn(self):
        tid = threading.get_ident()
        logging.info(f"{tid} - get connection")

        if tid not in self._pool:
            self._pool[tid] = Client(self._host, compression=self._compression, client_name=f"mlcoin-api-{tid}")
            logging.info(f"{tid} - new connection created")

        return self._pool[tid]

    async def execute(self, statement, values=None, types_check=True):
        def execute_in_pool(statement, values, types_check):
            tid = threading.get_ident()

            if tid not in self._pool:
                self._pool[tid] = Client(self._host, compression=self._compression, client_name=f"mlcoin-api-{tid}")
                logging.info(f"{tid} - new connection created")

            conn = self._pool[tid]
            return conn.execute(statement, values, types_check=types_check)

        execute_stmt = partial(execute_in_pool, types_check=True)
        result = await self._loop.run_in_executor(
            None,
            execute_stmt,
            statement,
            values)

        return result
```

issue_title: Accessing external app in websocket handler with Pulsar 1.2 throws TypeError
issue_body:
With Pulsar 1.1.3. we can access an external app using `yield from pulsar.get_application('app')` from a websocket handler. However, upon updating to pulsar 1.2, this same code throws ``TypeError: can't pickle generator objects``. Is this the expected behavior? If so, what is the correct design pattern to access an external app using a websocket handler?

Here is a simplified example of our websocket handler:

```python
import asyncio
import pulsar
from pulsar.apps import ws, wsgi


class Echo(ws.WS):

    def on_bytes(self, websocket, msg):
        websocket._loop.call_soon(
            pulsar.ensure_future, self._call(websocket, msg))

    @asyncio.coroutine
    def _call(self, websocket, msg):
        # This is actually an independent app, not wsgi
        app = yield from pulsar.get_application('wsgi')
        # Do some things with app
        websocket.write(msg)
        websocket.write_close()


class Site(wsgi.LazyWsgi):

    def setup(self, environ):
        return wsgi.WsgiHandler(
            [ws.WebSocket('/', Echo())])

def server(**kwargs):
    return wsgi.WSGIServer(callable=Site(), **kwargs)
```

The client uses aiohttp, like this:

```python 
import asyncio
import aiohttp

@asyncio.coroutine
def main():
    client = yield from aiohttp.ws_connect('http://127.0.0.1:8060/')
    client.send_bytes(b"hello")
    try:
        while True:
            resp = yield from client.receive()
            if resp.tp == aiohttp.MsgType.close:

            print(resp.data)
    finally:
        yield from client.close()

I'm also seeing the Monitoring thread now being _too fast_ and we're finalizing the same job twice. This comes from the fact we don't have some global register about what we've currently put into a queue to be finalized.

issue_title: AnalyzeSentiment of Language API should have "encoding type" as a flattened parameter
issue_body:
The [language_gapic.yaml](https://github.com/googleapis/googleapis/blob/master/google/cloud/language/v1/language_gapic.yaml) specifies the config information for the AnalyzeSentiment method. We can observe that the only parameter we flatten is "document". However, all the other methods (AnalyzeEntities and AnalyzeSyntax) have 2 parameters: "document" and "encoding_type".

We should update AnalyzeSentiment to also include "encoding_type" as a parameter to be flattened. There is nothing special about that method that would need to only have "document". This was confirmed by the API team.

But also, and more importantly, as a result of this, we have an odd situation where someone using the Language API will not be able to pass the encoding_type at all through our client (at least in java). Indeed, because in our config file only "document" is a flatten parameter, we decided to make the "request object" version of the method as private (Java example below). Therefore, users will not be able to call the AnalyzeSentiment method and specify the encoding_type. We should fix this!

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6609430/26268720/7e2f1b4e-3ca5-11e7-84c7-7bc1b15296dc.png)

And this is why the sample on cloud.google.com only specifies the "Document" parameter in the case of AnalyzeSentiment (and it can't call the request version of the method):

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6609430/26268756/9f97a986-3ca5-11e7-96e9-c14380460056.png)

But for AnalyzeEntities for instance, it can:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6609430/26268781/ca851d2c-3ca5-11e7-985f-6b9288149a39.png)

issue_title: Streamlink alternates between "stream" and "hls" for hls streams
issue_body:
Streamlink is mis-identifying hls streams.

Given the same exact hls stream (tested with foxsportsgo and watchespn), Streamlink will sometimes select "stream" for the plugin, other times it will select "hls" for the plugin.

The command line option given for URL starts with "hls://urltostream", so it should always be using the hls plugin.

this is causing stream failures when "stream" is selected instead, and it read-timeouts and exits.

example:

issue_title: Provide function to produce list of features from FeatureExtractionSettings object
issue_body: There should be an easy way to obtain a list of all features a FeatureExtractionSettings object is configured to calculate.

Version: latest

I tried to exclude some features from the extraction process but an error occured.
The error also occurs on running the example from http://tsfresh.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/tsfresh.feature_extraction.html#tsfresh.feature_extraction.settings.FeatureExtractionSettings:

```python
from tsfresh.feature_extraction import FeatureExtractionSettings
settings = FeatureExtractionSettings()
# Calculate all features except length
settings.do_not_calculate("length")
from tsfresh.feature_extraction import extract_features
extract_features(df, feature_extraction_settings=settings)
```
Follwoing exception is thrown:
```
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-19-39faeea46945> in <module>()
      2 settings = FeatureExtractionSettings()
      3 # Calculate all features except length
----> 4 settings.do_not_calculate("length")
      5 from tsfresh.feature_extraction import extract_features
      6 extract_features(df, feature_extraction_settings=settings)

TypeError: do_not_calculate() missing 1 required positional argument: 'identifier'
```

Anyway, the function `FeatureExtractionSettings.do_not_calculate()` cannot remove any features in that form, because at the position where the exception is thrown, the dictionary `settings.kind_to_calculation_settings_mapping`, which the functions tries to deduct items from, is empty.

For me, it make only sense, that the function `do_not_calculate()` is replaced by the following one:

```python
def do_not_calculate(self, kind):
        """
        Delete all features of type kind.

        :param kind: the type of the time series
        :type kind: basestring
        :return: The setting object itself
        :rtype: FeatureExtractionSettings
        """

        if not isinstance(kind, basestring):
            raise TypeError("Time series {} should be a string".format(kind))

        del self.name_to_param[kind]
        return self
```

I did not open a pull request, because the existing `FeatureExtractionSettings.do_not_calculate()` function has its eligibility to filter features after creating a settings object by calling `FeatureExtractionSettings.from_columns()`.

Can you check that please?

issue_title: Don't ask to temporarily shut down web server to pass challenge, instead make use of proxy_pass
issue_body:

letsencrypt uses port 80 to do the challenge/response thing https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/236af19a50de18a7cd831157dc53a6d22f79ee10/acme/acme/challenges.py#L223
It asks to temporarily stop an already running web server for it to bind its own web server to port 80 https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/d209b5ea20c2f5f35eadc304dbe5cbb85c233f4d/letsencrypt/plugins/standalone.py#L222

This causes downtime of the main http service. Downtime = bad.
Please offer an option to bind to another port and proxy_pass it to port 80. This will not cause any downtime.

这里我们采用HaProxy来做转发工具
首先安装HaProxy
Centos使用
```yum install haproxy```
Debian使用
```vi  /etc/apt/sources.list```
添加如下内容
```deb http://ftp.us.debian.org/debian/ wheezy-backports main```
然后
```apt-get update
apt-get install haproxy```
接下来设置配置文件
```vi /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg```
清空配置文件后，输入如下内容
```global
ulimit-n  51200
defaults
log global
mode    tcp
option  dontlognull
timeout connect 1000ms
timeout client 150000ms
timeout server 150000ms
listen status
bind 0.0.0.0:1080
mode http
log global
stats refresh 30s
stats uri /admin?stats
stats realm Private lands
stats auth admin:password
stats hide-version
frontend ssin
bind *:2000-3305
default_backend ssout
backend ssout
server server1 SS服务器的IP maxconn 204800
```
将里面的SS的服务器的IP替换成你自己的SS服务器的IP即可
其中的2000-3305为端口段，你可以改成你自己的端口段，我因为有mysql-server，所以3306无法被haproxy使用，只转发到了3305
如果需要多服务器轮询的可以在backend里多建几个server轮询。
保存退出后，
执行
```service haproxy restart```
即可使用

issue_title: Read video directly from MongoDB gridfs
issue_body:
Hi,
I've been trying to do video analysis reading files directly from a MongoDB gridFS storage using PIMS.

In a MongoDB gridfs document I can store very large files but I can also add metadata in Json format that I can use for data like calibration and sample specification and I can also store results from tracking back into the same json document so that when I retrieve the video I already have the tracking or any other analysis ready for use.

It works with CINE files with a very small modification to the open cine function, if I'm passing a gridout cursor, which is the file-like object returned by a mongodb query, I just skip the open function and from there everything works. I can ask for a specific frame without downloading the video.

Question for you, do you think this could be done also for other format like MOV, I see that you enable random access via pims but I think since the read function uses subprocess the mongodb filelike object is not accessible by the open function.

Hope this is clear enough for you to tell me if you already thought to some solution that would allow to keep files and data in a document based database and have pims accessing the frames without downloading the full video.

Thanks again for your libraries and all your nice work
    Marco

issue_title: Multiple inlines rendered for a single model when that model has another Many-to-Many relationship
issue_body:
## models.py

The team model can be assigned with a generic relationship to another model.

```python
class Team(models.Model):
	name = models.CharField(max_length=100)
	# Generic relationship
	content_type = models.ForeignKey(ContentType, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
	object_id = models.PositiveIntegerField()
	content_object = GenericForeignKey('content_type', 'object_id')

	class Meta:
		ordering = ['name']


class TeamRole(models.Model):
	role = models.ForeignKey(Role)
	team = models.ForeignKey(Team)
	members = models.ManyToManyField(User, blank=True)

	class Meta:
		ordering = ['team', 'role', 'members__first_name', 'members__last_name']

```

## admin.py
```python

class TeamRoleInline(nested_admin.NestedTabularInline):
	model = TeamRole
	extra = 0
	filter_horizontal = ['members', ]

class TeamInline(nested_admin.NestedGenericStackedInline):
	model = Team
	extra = 0
	inlines = [TeamRoleInline, ]
```

However, when adding multiple members to a TeamRole, multiple inline rows are rendered for that team role:

![screenshot](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2312145/16433051/12fffdd8-3d3d-11e6-99f8-853bd3ddb398.png)

In my serializer, to get around this issue:

```python
class TeamListSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
	roles = serializers.SerializerMethodField()

	class Meta:
		model = Team

	def get_roles(self, obj):
		return TeamRoleListSerializer(
			obj.teamrole_set.order_by('pk').distinct('pk'), many=True).data
```

https://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/latest/units-and-global-variables.html#abi-encoding-and-decoding-functions

abi.decode(bytes encodedData, (...)) returns (...): ABI-decodes the given data, while the types are given in parentheses as second argument. Example: (uint a, uint[2] memory b, bytes memory c) = abi.decode(data, (uint, uint[2], bytes))
abi.encode(...) returns (bytes): ABI-encodes the given arguments
abi.encodePacked(...) returns (bytes): Performs packed encoding of the given arguments
abi.encodeWithSelector(bytes4 selector, ...) returns (bytes): ABI-encodes the given arguments starting from the second and prepends the given four-byte selector

issue_title: Open files with O_EXCL in Python 2
issue_body:
`io.open` supports taking file descriptor instead of a file path (and can take ownership of the fd). `os.open` lets you open a file in exclusive mode and returns a file descriptor. You could do something like the following to open the file in the mode specified.

```
diff --git a/fs/osfs.py b/fs/osfs.py
index e53e5e2..9b2c330 100644
--- a/fs/osfs.py
+++ b/fs/osfs.py
@@ -422,8 +422,8 @@ class OSFS(FS):
         _path = self.validatepath(path)
         sys_path = self._to_sys_path(_path)
         with convert_os_errors('open', path):
-            if six.PY2 and _mode.exclusive and self.exists(path):
-                raise FileExists(path)
+            if six.PY2 and _mode.exclusive:
+                sys_path = os.open(sys_path, os.O_CREAT | os.O_EXCL)
             _encoding = encoding or 'utf-8'
             return io.open(
                 sys_path,
```

*(I tried running the tests to see what this breaks, but for some reason tox wouldn't stop barfing at me even on regular v2.0.24 without this change.)*

Proposed Enhancement:

For both the Consumer and Producer, it seems necessary to expose the error_cb in the Python API so that Consumer and Producer code can deal with errors, especially w.r.t. connections with Kafka.

For example, consider the Consumer pseudo code:

```
while True:
    msg = consumer.poll(1.0)
    if not msg:
        logger.info('No messages found in polling window')
        continue
    if msg.error():
        logger.exception('Received error', error=msg.error())
        raise confluent_kafka.KafkaException(msg.error())
```

This code will run forever, even if the consumer loses connection to the brokers, which isn't always what you want. In my case, I'd like to fail if the error has persisted for the past 5 minutes.

It seems like it wouldn't be too much work to do something similar to what is done for the `on_revoke` and `on_assign` callbacks, but not sure about the intricacies of the librdkafka's error_cb.

This is blocking us from adopting confluent-kafka-python more broadly (or at all).

Thanks for your time.

issue_title: cot verification: a decision task shouldn't add its decision task to the chain
issue_body:
In [bug 1338933](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1338933), I retriggered a decision task.

The [original decision task](https://tools.taskcluster.net/task-group-inspector/#/N-cSl1F8Shux4rgxCP4QHg/N-cSl1F8Shux4rgxCP4QHg?_k=6k2aob) had a matching `taskId` and `taskGroupId`, because it was created through cron.yml.

The [retriggered decision task](https://tools.taskcluster.net/task-inspector/#C5_wx697TsydQOxUWH9vbg/) had mismatched `taskId` and `taskGroupId`, because it was retriggered with the `taskGroupId` from the original decision task.

However, if the retriggered decision task is otherwise well-formed and pointing at a valid tree, we shouldn't need to trace it back to the original decision task.

I'm thinking we should put in an exception to the `taskGroupId` rule: if a task is a decision task, don't trace it back to its original decision task.  This assumes that all decision tasks are traceable back to the tree and `mach taskgraph` currently.  If that changes, then we'll have to revisit this rule.

issue_title: Ensure that `smoketest` uses an unused port for ethereum rpc
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The current `smoketest` implementation has a stub implementation `get_free_port` that always returns the first port of the pre setup port range. The idea of `get_free_port` is to chose a free port during the smoketest.

## Solution

Either change the implementation of `smoketest.py::get_free_port` to actually check for a free port, or use a fixed port and add a user friendly error condition for a port clash.

## Problem Definition

Pagination is done either with filters for the block number or ids, neither of these filters allow the caller to control how many entries will be returned.

The API client cannot know how many events happen for a given block, so the number of entries is uncertain, and not all entries are returned, so pages filtered by ids may have holes in the response.

## Proposed solution

Change the rest API to include a `limit` and `offset` filters, with these filters the following semantics must hold:

- Elements must always have contiguous positions. This implies that a new request with offset starting at the previous offset + number of entries returned, will not return repeated elements.
- The API must return at most `limit` elements

## Task list

- [ ] Add to state changes and events sqlite tables a column to identify if the element should be expose through the APIs or not. 
- [ ] Update the raiden events API
- [ ] Update the channel events API

issue_title: Running workflows docs - example should be more intuitive
issue_body:
@bocklund 

The goal of the Running Workflows docs is to demonstrate atomate feeling "magical" - i.e., things that are normally difficult become super easy. i.e., the steps should be short and intuitive. This is our primary example to demonstrate why someone spent so much time installing the software. After that, the rest should be easy.

The example doesn't really demonstrate that.

1. If I were a new user, setting up the eos workflow wouldn't seem like something I could do myself. That YAML file is super complicated containing all sorts of foreign terms like "TransmuterFW" that a new user will have no clue about. And it's also really long and tedious - not exactly short and tidy. I have no idea where to start in terms of looking.
2. If I were a new user, analyzing the eos workflow wouldn't seem like something I could do myself. It seems to require intimate knowledge of what's being put in the database - field names, etc. - and even after that requires quite a bit of extra coding on my part. Feels more like manual labor that requires a *lot* of prior knowledge than magical. And I don't even get the fit of the equation of state at the end, despite the workflow name! So it's pretty much a letdown.

A better example would be:

1. Add a band structure workflow with one line. Either of the below

atwf add Si.cif -p wf_bandstructure
OR
atwf add Si.cif -l vasp -s bandstructure.yaml
OR
atwf add -m mp-149 -p wf_bandstructure

You can take the opportunity to point out all the other preset workflows that are available as Python functions (option 1) or YAML (option 2). e.g., if you just want to optimize, use the optimize_only.yaml file. Plus the YAML file is super clean for those. Also point out that they have access to 60,000 crystal structures if they use the -m option. A newcomer won't think the code is about having huge YAML specifications that are for expert coders.

2. Run using:
qlaunch -r rapidfire

They already met this command in the install tutorial. If they have the ability to run locally (not everyone does, e.g. NERSC officially doesn't allow this even if you can technically do it), then you can point them to rlaunch as well as a vasp_cmd that is more basic (see my other ticket about this)

3. Analyze and plot using pymatgen. I'd suggest something like:
1. Use VaspCalcDb to connect to MongoDB
2. Use the get_band_structure() and get_dos() methods of the above to get band structure and dos objects
3. print out a few attributes of the above objects like band gap or fermi energy
4. Use BSDosPlotter() to produce a pretty plot

All 4 of the above steps can probably be done in 10 lines of code or less - with each line being very short and clear about what it's doing (almost like English).

If there are more complex analysis that require digging into the database, you can demonstrate those too. But it shouldn't be the first thing the user is exposed to.

For the current EOS example, I'd suggest:
- encouraging a preset workflow instead of the YAML format, which misses the all-important fitting portion (and one of the great benefits of using atomate)
- having the analysis be little more than querying the database for the result or inspecting the JSON file in the result directory. You can also give an example of manual analysis for the brave (retain the current analysis) but again one should not get the impression that this is what is expected.

issue_title: social_core setup.py may detect wrong python version when using pipenv
issue_body:
With the new pipenv, which is [recommended by python.org now](https://packaging.python.org/new-tutorials/installing-and-using-packages/#installing-pipenv), there are issues with using os.sys_version in setup.py to determine which dependencies to install.

It appears that since pipenv technically runs in your main python environment rather than inside the virtualenv it manages, setup.py detects that python version.  So for example I use python 3.6 as my main python, but have a few legacy projects which are on 2.7 for now.  If I use pipenv then social_core's setup.py sees python 3.6 in os.sys_version and pops python3-openid into the requirements rather than python-openid.

Would it be possible to use the `extras_require` feature of setuptools 18.0 and up to handle the conditional dependency?

Please also see https://github.com/kennethreitz/pipenv/issues/924

issue_title: Allow returning values from transactional functions
issue_labels: [{'id': 539494117, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20spanner', 'name': 'api: spanner', 'color': 'cd7f32', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604235, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/status:%20acknowledged', 'name': 'status: acknowledged', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438580, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'db4437', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Somebody asks in the Slack:

> Question about spanner python APIs, specifically transactions. 
> In a read-write transaction, is it possible to return the updated
> value without hitting the database again?
> 
> let's say I have the following transaction (pretty much the example from the docs)
> ```def f(transaction):
>     second_album_result = transaction.read(
>             table='Albums', columns=('MarketingBudget',),
>             keyset=second_album_keyset, limit=1)
>     second_album_row = list(second_album_result)[0]
>     second_album_budget = second_album_row[0]
>     second_album_budget += 10000000
>     transaction.update(table=mytable, columns=('MarketingBudget'),
>                         keyset=second_album_keyset, values=[(second_album_budget)]
>                     )  
>     return second_album_budget
> database.run_in_transaction(f)
> ```
> is the `return` at all possible?

I believe that this is currently not possible in the Python library, because when you pass a closure to `run_in_transaction`, the return value always gets ignored, and it just returns the commit timestamp.

However this _is_ possible in the Cloud Spanner Java library, and being be able to return whatever you want from the body of a transactional function in the case that a transaction successfully commits is extremely useful.
